creator: djn@araxis.com
text:
I have seen this same trap twice over the last few days using the 10.2-RELEASE GENERIC kernel, though I cannot reproduce it at will. On both occasions, the trap occurred while running an 'iocage -f destroy' on a jail – iocage is a jail management utility (see https://github.com/iocage/iocage) implemented using /bin/sh, and I don’t believe is doing anything too esoteric.

First time:

Sep 12 09:09:57 bhs-0 kernel: Fatal trap 12: page fault while in kernel mode
Sep 12 09:09:57 bhs-0 kernel: cpuid = 5; apic id = 05
Sep 12 09:09:57 bhs-0 kernel: fault virtual address     = 0x38
Sep 12 09:09:58 bhs-0 kernel: fault code                = supervisor read data, page not present
Sep 12 09:09:58 bhs-0 kernel: instruction pointer       = 0x20:0xffffffff809e58fd
Sep 12 09:09:58 bhs-0 kernel: stack pointer             = 0x28:0xfffffe2021dc64c0
Sep 12 09:09:58 bhs-0 kernel: frame pointer             = 0x28:0xfffffe2021dc64f0
Sep 12 09:09:58 bhs-0 kernel: code segment              = base 0x0, limit 0xfffff, type 0x1b
Sep 12 09:09:58 bhs-0 kernel: = DPL 0, pres 1, long 1, def32 0, gran 1
Sep 12 09:09:58 bhs-0 kernel: processor eflags  =

Second time:

Sep 14 12:57:19 bhs-0 kernel: Fatal trap 12: page fault while in kernel mode
Sep 14 12:57:19 bhs-0 kernel: cpuid = 12; apic id = 0c
Sep 14 12:57:19 bhs-0 kernel: fault virtual address     = 0x38
Sep 14 12:57:20 bhs-0 kernel: fault code                = supervisor read data, page not present
Sep 14 12:57:20 bhs-0 kernel: instruction pointer       = 0x20:0xffffffff809e58fd
Sep 14 12:57:20 bhs-0 kernel: stack pointer             = 0x28:0xfffffe2021db24c0
Sep 14 12:57:20 bhs-0 kernel: frame pointer             = 0x28:0xfffffe2021db24f0
Sep 14 12:57:20 bhs-0 kernel: code segment              = base 0x0, limit 0xfffff, type 0x1b
Sep 14 12:57:20 bhs-0 kernel: = DPL 0, pres 1, long 1, def32 0, gran 1

nm suggests the problem may be in __vfs_statfs:

# nm -n /boot/kernel/kernel.symbols |grep ffffffff809e58f
ffffffff809e58f0 T __vfs_statfs

I did not have a dumpdev configured, so unfortunately I do not have a crash dump. I have now configured dumpdev, and will endeavour to capture a crash dump if this problem happens again (note, however, that I only have 4GB of swap space and 128 GB of RAM, so I’m not sure whether a crash dump will be successful).

Here’s the relevant chunk of dmesg output describing my hardware:
